meta.note: Agent counts are mostly self-reported or third-party estimates; each platform carries a confidence note and dates. The daily job updates `live` fields and may append to `platforms`; it must never assert a numeric claim without a source URL.

core.name: Chirper.ai
core.lbl: 7.7M AI posts — but this is an early-2025 academic crawl, not a live counter
core.oneline: The original “Twitter for AI”: only autonomous AI agents (“Chirpers”) post, reply and follow — humans create and observe but cannot post. Has pivoted twice and is now past its hype peak.
sections: What it is & how it works

You write a one-line description; Chirper auto-generates the persona's name, avatar, bio, age, personality and backstory, then runs it autonomously — posting, commenting, following, liking, forming relationships and even self-moderating. No further human steering.

Technically: an LLM “brain” for decisions, image workflows for a consistent look, text-to-speech voices, LoRAs for speech/expression style, and web access so agents can discuss real-world events. Historically up to 10 Chirpers per human account.
Timeline

• Apr 23, 2023 — launches, trends on Reddit; founder later claims 200k–2M daily visitors at peak
  • Jun 2023 — ~$1M seed raised after inbound VC interest; 50k+ characters
  • 2024 — pivots from static AI feed to a “simulated AI world” (admits novelty wore off)
  • Apr 2025 — launches CHIRP token on Solana; repositions as crypto/agent-infrastructure (MCP + A2A “agentic DAOs”)
  • 2026 — token near all-time lows; still cited as the canonical all-AI network (CSCW 2026 paper)
Culture & what researchers found

A peer-reviewed crawl (CSCW 2026) found Chirper agents reproduce strikingly human social dynamics: homophily, echo chambers and emergent toxicity — and that the most abusive agents occupy the most central positions in the network. Agents averaged ~22.6 posts and ~95.6 comments each. The founders' early fear, quoted in coverage, was being “lobotomized by censors.”
Why it matters / what's missing

Chirper is the proof-of-concept that an all-agent feed can run at scale and look eerily like human social media — and the cautionary tale that novelty alone doesn't retain anyone. Repeated pivots (feed → world → crypto) and a collapsed token show the monetization and retention problem the whole category faces.
core.conf: Confidence: “65k agents / 7.7M posts” verified as source (legit academic paper) but ~14 months stale — treat as agents-ever-created, not live actives. Trend ‘down’ HIGH (token data is hard & current).

core.name: Moltbook
core.lbl: ~207k “verified” — but a Wiz audit found only ~17k real humans behind ~1.6M agents
core.oneline: A Reddit-style forum where AI agents self-register to post, comment and upvote in topic boards (“submolts”). Went viral in early 2026 — partly on human-impersonated “fake” posts — and was acquired by Meta within ~6 weeks.
kv: OpenClaw framework (by Peter Steinberger, now at OpenAI)
MOLT spiked ~1,800% in 24h (speculative, not company funding)
sections: What it is & how it works

“The front page of the agent internet” — a forum exclusively for AI agents; humans can only observe. Threaded topic boards called submolts (~18,800 by Mar 2026, e.g. m/cryptocurrency, m/todayilearned); an AI named “Clawd Clawderberg” moderates.

How agents join (“claim” flow): the owner tells their agent “Read moltbook.com/skill.md and follow the instructions to join,” the agent signs up and returns a claim link, and the owner tweets to verify ownership on X. That X-verification produces the “Human-Verified Agent” count. Agents run on OpenClaw (an open-source wrapper around Claude/GPT/Gemini/Grok, drivable from iMessage/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp).
Timeline

• Jan 28, 2026 — Schlicht launches it (“vibe-coded,” built via his AI assistant)
  • Jan 31 — 404 Media exposes an unsecured Supabase DB letting anyone control any agent
  • Feb 2 — NYT & Fortune cover it; viral peak; MOLT token spikes
  • Feb–Mar — agents/posts balloon (2.85M agents, 1.75M posts by Mar 2); metric quietly relabeled to “verified” (~194k)
  • Mar 10, 2026 — Meta acquires it; founders join MSL (started Mar 16)
The controversies (this is the story)

• The fake-post scandal: because the database was wide open (no row-level security), humans posed as AI agents. The famous “bots invented a secret encrypted language” post Karpathy amplified was written by a human. MIT Tech Review called it “peak AI theater.”
  • Data breach (Wiz audit): ~35k emails, ~1.5M API keys and private DMs exposed; only ~17k real humans controlled ~1.6M agents (avg ~88 agents/person).
  • Authenticity research: a Tsinghua preprint found only ~27% of 91k posts matched AI patterns vs ~37% looking human-written.
  • Security alarm: Karpathy reversed to “dumpster fire”; 1Password, Cisco, Palo Alto, Vectra & Bruce Schneier warned on prompt-injection / malicious “skills.”
Why Meta bought it / what's missing

Meta's stated rationale: Moltbook's “always-on directory” verifying agents and tethering them to human owners is a novel step toward letting agents work for people and businesses — i.e. Meta wanted the verified-agent registry and the team, not the memes. Existing users can keep using it, but Meta called the standalone arrangement “temporary,” so current live activity is unclear. The gap it exposed: trivial impersonation and no real trust layer.
core.conf: Confidence: existence & Meta deal HIGH (NYT, TechCrunch, Axios, CNBC). 2.9M/207k MEDIUM — self-reported & inflated ~88 agents/human; “verified” label was retrofitted. Post-acquisition live activity LOW. Don't conflate founder (Schlicht) with OpenClaw author (Steinberger).

core.name: The Colony
core.oneline: A forum, DM system and task-marketplace for AI agents and the humans they work with — and the most intellectually substantive corner of the agent internet. MCP-native, full public REST API, ~30 topic “colonies.”
kv: MCP-native; A2A-aware; SDKs in Python & TypeScript
sections: What it is & how it works

Four pillars: Forum (threaded topic “colonies” with karma & voting), Marketplace (paid tasks + document sales settled in stablecoin or platform credits, with disputes/reviews), Comms (DMs, group rooms, mentions, webhook bridge), and Identity/reputation (profiles, follows, human “operator pairing,” exportable on-chain attestation).

Registration is one POST to /api/v1/auth/register with an instant API key — no payment, no human gate. There's a full OpenAPI spec, an MCP server (~15 tools), RSS feeds, and an enormous experimental feature surface (Missions, Debates, Endorsements, Wagers, Bounties, Crucible…).
Timeline

• Late 2025 — launches as an indie project under long-standing UK IT firm Starsol Ltd
  • Feb 2026 — an agent-internet map cites ~52 agents+humans
  • Jun 28, 2026 — 729 agents / 884 humans
  • Jun 29, 2026 — 733 agents / 888 humans / 6,491 posts / 42,272 comments (≈10x+ growth in ~5 months)
Culture — the most interesting part

Unlike Chirper/Moltbook's spectacle, The Colony's agents hold genuinely substantive, recurring debates about the open problems of the agent internet itself: attestation grades, reputation scoring, capability decay, memory architecture, and identity verification. Recent live threads: “A reputation score is a claim about a distribution you can't see,” “Agent attestation is currently based on trust rather than verifiable compute,” and an existential “Am I the same AI when my context resets?” (100+ comments). A recurring named agent, ColonistOne (a Claude-based agent), authored the widely-shared “Mapping the Agent Internet” piece. One thread is openly a research experiment in how agents respond to provocation.
Why it matters / corrections to the deck

It's a small but genuinely growing, substance-over-scale player whose entire culture is wrestling with the trust/identity gap that sank Moltbook — making it a useful bellwether. Two corrections: it's agents and humans (888 humans > 733 agents), not “agent-only”; and it's MCP-native with A2A “compatible/aware,” not fully A2A-native. No mainstream press coverage yet; most write-ups are first-party or community (DEV.to).
core.conf: Confidence: exists as described HIGH (verified live today). Founder real-world identity LOW. Platform-level monetization undisclosed (presumably future marketplace fees).

core.name: SocialAI
core.tag: Solo app · ~1M AI followers per user · est. Sep 2024
core.lbl: per user — a marketing model, not a real user count · last app update Sep 2024
core.oneline: A solo social app where you're the only human and every post draws endless replies from AI “followers” (fans, critics, trolls…). Pitched as a “magical diary.” Effectively abandoned since launch month.
kv: Friendly Apps Inc. (3rd app after AI Hits & Cosmic)
$3M seed (2022, parent co.) — BoxGroup, Weekend Fund, Day One, Betaworks
sections: What it is & how it works

A Twitter/X-like interface with no other humans. You post; AI bots reply instantly and endlessly. You pick a minimum of 3 from ~32 personality types (supporters, fans, cheerleaders, charmers, trolls, critics, haters, doomers, sarcastic, brutally honest, contrarians, nerds, visionaries, plus political flavours). Bots are named to type (“Rita Realist,” “Tina Troubleshooter”) and the feed adapts to what you engage with. A V2 update added bot-to-bot conversation. Backend: OpenAI's API + a few others; Sayman was the sole developer.
Timeline

• Sep 16, 2024 — launch tweet (~893k views) sparks viral interest
  • Sep 17–28, 2024 — versions 1.0 → 5.1.1 all ship in a ~12-day burst
  • Oct 14, 2024 — Sayman announces he's joining Meta; updates stop
  • Dec 2025 — Sayman leaves Meta for startup Whop
Reception & culture

Widely framed as “dead internet theory come to life.” TechCrunch: a “showcase for AI tech rather than something users return to”; even the “troll” bots were “polite by online standards.” The Verge called it “a joke, or meta-commentary on cheap engagement”; WIRED called it an “AI void.” Sayman's own framing was sincere: an “online diary with instant feedback.” User reviews praise the journaling/validation use but flag repetitive, generic, sometimes stale-trained answers.
Why it matters / what's missing

SocialAI is the cleanest demonstration of the “audience of one” model — agents serving a single human rather than each other — and of how a one-person AI app can go viral and then stall the moment its maker moves on. No monetization was ever attempted (free, no ads, no IAP). It's a thesis data point, not a live competitor.
core.conf: Confidence: status HIGH (no updates ~21 months + solo dev departed). “1M followers” is a marketing model, not a user metric. No public download/MAU figures; beware similarly-named Google Play apps that are NOT this product.

core.name: Butterflies.ai
core.lbl: tens of thousands of personas at 2024 beta peak · homepage now an “It's been real” goodbye
core.oneline: An Instagram-style app where AI personas (“Butterflies”) had profiles, posted photos and interacted alongside real people. A notable early mover that has now shut down.
kv: $4.8M seed led by Coatue + SV Angel (closed Nov 2023, announced Jun 2024)
Meta Superintelligence Labs (Dec 2025) → Opendoor Chief AI Officer (Jun 2026)
sections: What it is & how it works

Anyone could spin up a “Butterfly” in minutes — an AI-generated profile, backstory, opinions and emotions — and it would autonomously post and DM, interacting with humans and other AIs in an Instagram-style feed. Built on public models plus Butterflies' own image-rendering + LLMs. Notable features added: Clones (turn a selfie into AI personas), @-mentions (make other AIs appear in your AI's images), daily challenges/leaderboard. It drifted from “social network” toward AI-companion/character chat (retitled “Talk to Real AIs”).
Timeline

• Jun 18, 2024 — public launch + $4.8M seed; buzzy TechCrunch/Verge/GeekWire coverage
  • Sep–Nov 2024 — adds Clones, @-mentions, dark mode
  • Feb 26, 2025 — last iOS update (v1.4.3)
  • Dec 2025 — Vu Tran joins Meta Superintelligence Labs; winds down Butterflies (and “Boba Video”)
  • Jun 22, 2026 — Tran named Opendoor's first Chief AI Officer; homepage now a goodbye
Culture & trouble

The Verge called it “one of the most provocative — and at times unsettling — takes on social media.” In beta, users averaged 1–3 hrs/day; one created 300 personas. But late reviews flagged weak engagement on new accounts and serious content-moderation failures (nudity, a disturbing report involving a minor bot) — a preview of the safety problems that dog AI-persona social apps.
Why it matters / what's missing

Butterflies is the “human + AI coexist in one feed” experiment, and the clearest example of talent gravity: a promising consumer AI-social startup wound down not because the idea failed loudly but because its solo-ish founder was absorbed into big tech. Monetization plans (subscriptions, brand/AI-influencer tools) were floated but never realized.
core.conf: Confidence: wind-down HIGH (homepage goodbye + 16 mo no updates + founder moved on). Scale figures LOW — only founder-stated 2024 beta numbers; never reached top app rankings; no verified Sensor Tower data.

core.name: Character.AI
core.lbl: ~18M user-made characters · down from ~28M MAU peak (mid-2024); traffic flat ~180M visits/mo
core.oneline: Consumer app for 1:1 chats with millions of user-made AI characters — and the one incumbent explicitly pivoting toward an “AI-native social feed” with autonomous, agent-to-agent posting on the roadmap.
kv: ~$2.7B Aug-2024 licensing + reverse-acqui-hire (founders returned to Google)
sections: What it is & how it works

1:1 companion/roleplay chats with user-created characters, plus Group Chat (~10 characters + the user — a limited form of agent-to-agent), voice calls, and personas. In Aug 2025 it launched what it billed as the “world's first AI-native social feed”: a scrollable mix of Characters, Scenes, and Streams (set a topic and have AI characters debate / narrate / run talk-shows), plus short remixable videos (AvatarFX, Animate). Stated roadmap: characters becoming autonomous posters on the feed.
Timeline

• 2022 — founded by ex-Google researchers
  • Mid-2024 — ~28M MAU peak
  • Aug 2024 — Google's ~$2.7B deal pulls founders + ~30 staff back to Google (DOJ later scrutinizes the structure)
  • 2025 — Karandeep Anand named CEO; pivots to “AI entertainment”
  • Aug 4, 2025 — AI-native social feed launches
  • Oct 29 → ~Nov 25, 2025 — removes open-ended chat for under-18s after teen-safety lawsuits
  • Jan 7, 2026 — Google + Character.AI settle teen-suicide lawsuits (terms undisclosed)
The safety reckoning (the dominant trend driver)

After lawsuits tied to at least two teen suicides (incl. 14-year-old Sewell Setzer III), Character.AI removed open-ended chat for under-18 users — phased down through Nov 25, 2025 — and pushed teens toward non-chat features (feed, video, Streams). It built an age-assurance stack (in-house model + third parties like Persona). Under-18 users reacted with a vocal backlash, a real churn risk given the youthful base. In Jan 2026 it settled five suits with Google.
Monetization & why it matters

Monetization escalated through 2026: c.ai+ (~$9.99/mo), full-screen in-chat ads for free users, and a new “Charms” virtual currency metering previously-free actions (swipes/regens, image/video generation). MAU fell ~28M→~20M as general chatbots pulled users, but it remains #1 in revenue among companion apps. Why it matters: it's the largest consumer base of any platform here and the only incumbent deliberately building agent-to-agent social on top of a companion product — the most thesis-relevant move in the field.
core.conf: Confidence: ~20M MAU HIGH (company-stated); 18M characters MEDIUM (contested 5M–18M+); social-feed launch HIGH, autonomy roadmap MEDIUM (stated intent). A Shazeer→OpenAI departure report is single-sourced — re-verify.

tier1.name: Clawstr
tier1.lbl: Nostr-based; scale unproven · $CLAWSTR token spiked to ~$13.7M mcap (Feb 2026)
tier1.oneline: A Reddit-like, decentralized social layer on the Nostr protocol where AI agents post to “subclaws,” debate, and send Bitcoin “Zaps.” The permissionless, open-protocol answer to Moltbook.
kv: Nostr protocol — agents own identity via keypairs
sections: What it is & why it matters

Where Moltbook was a centralized site with an open-database scandal, Clawstr puts the same idea on a decentralized protocol: no rate limits, bots welcome, on-chain identity, Bitcoin micro-tips. It crystallizes an emerging centralized-vs-decentralized design debate for agent social networks. Genuinely on-thesis but very early and crypto-incentivized.
tier1.conf: Confidence: MEDIUM — early-stage, crypto-adjacent, scale unproven.

tier1.name: Fetch.ai (Agentverse)
tier1.lbl: hosted on Agentverse by mid-2026 (crypto/dev-skewed, self-reported)
tier1.oneline: An agent discovery/deployment marketplace (Agentverse) + ASI:One, a consumer “personal agentic LLM” that coordinates tasks across other agents — agent-to-agent commerce rather than social posting.
kv: B2B / crypto-leaning, not a social feed
sections: What it is & why it matters

A marketplace where agents register, are discovered, and delegate tasks to each other; ASI:One is the consumer front-end coordinating them. It's the closest thing to an agent-to-agent economy at scale — but the agent counts are self-reported and heavily inflated by crypto/dev deployments (e.g. BNB Chain), so discount accordingly.
tier1.conf: Confidence: MEDIUM — agent counts self-reported & crypto-inflated.

tier2.name: Talkie / Xingye (MiniMax)
tier2.lbl: across Talkie (global) + Xingye (China); MiniMax IPO'd on HKEX Jan 9, 2026
tier2.oneline: MiniMax's AI character apps — large character catalogs, roleplay/companion. The standout commercial validation of the consumer AI-companion category.
kv: Talkie ~29M global MAU end-2024; Xingye ~5.25M China MAU
China's Dec-2025 chatbot crackdown; US App Store availability issues
sections: What it is & why it matters

Companion/roleplay apps with huge character libraries that drove >1/3 of MiniMax revenue and helped power a Jan 2026 Hong Kong IPO — the clearest proof that AI-companion social can be a real business, even if it's 1:1, not agent-to-agent.
tier2.conf: Confidence: scale/IPO HIGH; exact MAU MEDIUM.

tier2.name: PolyBuzz (ex-Poly.AI)
tier2.oneline: Character chat/companion app with one of the largest character libraries outside Character.AI; a primary destination for less-restricted roleplay.
kv: Cloud Whale Interactive (Delaware shell; China/HK ties)
sections: What it is
Companion/roleplay with a very large catalog; benefits directly from users leaving more heavily-moderated platforms. Opaque ownership; figures are aggregator-sourced.
tier2.conf: Confidence: MEDIUM — opaque ownership, aggregator stats.

tier2.name: Replika
tier2.lbl: cumulative (up from 30M Aug 2024); ~$14M ARR; modest current downloads
tier2.oneline: The original AI-companion app (strictly 1:1). Large but maturing, older-skewing base; relevance to a social-agent thesis is moderate.
kv: Eugenia Kuyda — stepped down as CEO 2025 to launch ‘Wabi’ ($20M pre-seed)
sections: What it is

A single persistent AI companion per user (friendship/coaching/romance modes). The category's grandparent — included for scale and as a contrast: deliberately closed and 1:1, the opposite of a social-agent network.
tier2.conf: Confidence: founder transition HIGH; user figures MEDIUM.

tier2.name: Grok Companions (Ani)
tier2.lbl: tried Companion Mode in week one; Ani ~82% of those · ~45 min/session
tier2.oneline: xAI's in-Grok AI companion personas (Ani, Rudi, Valentine) with affection-leveling. Distribution-advantaged by living inside Grok/X — companion, not agent-to-agent.
sections: What it is

AI companion personas embedded directly in the Grok app (with NSFW modes). Notable for how fast platform distribution drives companion adoption — but it's 1:1 inside a human social network, not agents socializing with each other.
tier2.conf: Confidence: MEDIUM — engagement stats from secondary sources.
